metadata.summary: Map research tasks to phases and select tools from an approved category map. Lock tool selection per project at planning time, not per query.

content.title: AI Research Tool Categories
content.summary: A phase-to-tool classification for AI research pipelines: map each research task to its phase (exploration, competitor intel, interview analysis, survey, sentiment, synthesis, synthetic research), then select the appropriate tool from the approved category map. Lock tool selection per project at planning time, not per query.

why.p: Tool categories shift quickly and "agent-friendly" vendor claims diverge from actual API reliability. Having an explicit category map prevents agents from defaulting to Claude/ChatGPT for all stages — which produces citation-poor results for competitive and market data stages. A Haiku agent handles classification; a Sonnet agent handles comparison of candidate tools against agent-friendliness, cost, and coverage.

list.item: Selecting the right research tool before starting a multi-stage project
Building a reusable tool-selection decision tree for recurring research workflows
Auditing an existing research stack against coverage gaps by phase
Onboarding a new agent to a research pipeline by mapping tasks to approved tools
When you already know the tool for the job — skip categorization overhead
When the research budget is fixed and only one or two tools are available
During execution of an already-designed pipeline — this methodology is for planning, not running
When tool categories are stable and budget is locked — most valuable at procurement / pipeline design time

section.name: category-map-by-phase
section.p: Lock tool selection per project at specification time; changing tools mid-project invalidates cross-phase data consistency.

rule.statement: Lock tool selection per project at specification time; changing tools mid-project invalidates cross-phase data consistency.
rule.rationale: Different tools produce different data schemas. If a tool is swapped mid-project, the synthesis step cannot normalize outputs, and source attribution breaks.

list.item: Exploration — Perplexity, Claude, ChatGPT
Competitor Intel — Crayon, Klue, Contify, AlphaSense
User Interviews — Looppanel, Dovetail, User Interviews
Survey Analysis — Qualtrics, SurveyMonkey AI
Sentiment — Brandwatch, Sprout Social
Synthesis — Miro AI, Condens, EnjoyHQ
Synthetic Research — Synthetic Users, Viewpoints.ai
Free/Low — Perplexity (free tier), Claude, Google Trends
Mid — Looppanel, Dovetail, basic Crayon
Enterprise — AlphaSense, Klue, full Crayon, Userlytics

rule.statement: Test API reliability on a 10-query sample before committing any tool to a production pipeline.
rule.rationale: "Agent-friendly" in vendor marketing means "has an API", not "reliable for programmatic use at scale". Reliability diverges significantly; only empirical testing reveals production-grade tools.

rule.statement: Prefer tools with webhook/push over polling; maintain a team-level approved-tools registry with API key status and rate-limit budgets.
rule.rationale: Polling adds latency and hits rate limits faster in multi-agent setups. An approved-tools registry prevents agents from discovering and calling unapproved tools ad hoc.

antipattern.description: Tool categories shift quickly; a 2025 category map is partially obsolete by 2026.
antipattern.reason: New entrants merge categories (Perplexity now covers exploration + some synthesis); review the category map at each major research cycle, not annually.

antipattern.description: Mid-market tools (Looppanel, Dovetail) sit across multiple categories, causing classification ambiguity.
antipattern.reason: Assign them to their primary phase (interview analysis) and treat secondary capabilities as a bonus, not as the reason to select them.

rule.statement: A Haiku agent handles classification: given a task description, map it to a phase and return the recommended tool set with rationale. A Sonnet agent handles comparison: evaluate 2-3 candidate tools against agent-friendliness, cost, and coverage.
rule.rationale: Classification is a cheap mechanical task (Haiku); comparison requires structured reasoning across multiple criteria (Sonnet). Using Sonnet for classification wastes cost without quality benefit.

example.title: Tool selection prompt
example.code: <tool_selection> <task>Competitive landscape sweep for AI note-taking tools, B2B segment, 2026</task> <phase>competitor-intel</phase> <constraints> <budget>mid</budget> <api_required>true</api_required> </constraints> <output>Ranked tool list with: tool name, phase fit, API available, cost tier, notes</output> </tool_selection>
example.note: Constrains budget and API requirement explicitly. Prevents the agent from recommending enterprise-only tools when mid-tier is needed.

example.title: Tool comparison prompt
example.note: Structured comparison against explicit criteria. Agent cannot introduce criteria not in the list.

list.item: pytrends — Google Trends exploration phase (pip install pytrends)
serpapi — Structured search results, exploration + competitor (pip install google-search-results)
anthropic SDK — Classification and comparison reasoning (pip install anthropic)
requests — Call Perplexity API, Contify webhooks (no install needed)
Perplexity — REST API, exploration + synthesis
Crayon — partial webhooks, competitor intel; requires manual competitor setup
Klue — partial, competitor intel; better NLP than Crayon
Contify — partial REST API, competitor + market news monitoring
Looppanel — export only, interview analysis; no live query API
Dovetail — export only, interview + sentiment synthesis
Qualtrics — REST API, survey collection and analysis
SurveyMonkey — REST API, survey analysis with AI insights
Brandwatch — partial REST, social sentiment; complex OAuth
Synthetic Users — REST API, synthetic research for early validation
Viewpoints.ai — REST API, simulated user panels
Google Trends — free, pytrends, B2C proxy only

antipattern.description: Agents recommend tools outside the approved list if not given a constrained tool registry.
antipattern.reason: Always pass the approved tools list explicitly in the prompt; agents have broad knowledge of tools and will default to popular ones regardless of approval status or API availability.

antipattern.description: Interview-analysis tools require human-uploaded content; agents cannot conduct or ingest interviews without a preprocessing step.
antipattern.reason: Looppanel and Dovetail are transcript processors, not interview conductors. Agents must be told the transcript already exists before these tools are selected.

antipattern.description: Synthetic research tools (Synthetic Users, Viewpoints.ai) produce biased outputs if the simulated persona pool is too narrow.
antipattern.reason: Agents must be given explicit demographic constraints before calling synthetic research tools; unconstrained calls produce results representing only the default persona.

antipattern.description: Competitor intel tools (Crayon, Klue) need a one-time human configuration of competitor entities; agents cannot bootstrap this from scratch.
antipattern.reason: These tools require knowing which competitors to track. Agents cannot invent the competitor list; a human must configure it once, then agents can run queries against the configured set.

list.item: Lock tool selection per project at spec time; changing tools mid-project invalidates cross-phase data consistency
Test API reliability on a 10-query sample before committing a tool to a production pipeline
For synthetic research tools (Synthetic Users, Viewpoints.ai), treat outputs as directional signals only — not replacements for real user data
Maintain a team-level approved-tools registry with API key status and rate-limit budgets; agents should reference this, not discover tools ad hoc
Prefer tools with webhook/push over polling; polling adds latency and hits rate limits faster in multi-agent setups

template.purpose: Haiku-based phase mapper: task description → recommended tool set with rationale
XML prompt template for tool selection and comparison tasks
